feed.title: vonMentzer-Group
feed.subtitle: Website of the von Mentzer Group at the University of Gothenburg, led by Dr Astrid von Mentzer.

entry.title: Welcome Gleb Ebert to the von Mentzer Lab

link.title: Welcome Gleb Ebert to the von Mentzer Lab

entry.content:
I am excited to welcome Gleb Ebert to the von Mentzer Lab as a postdoctoral researcher.

Gleb joins us from ETH Zürich, where he worked on computational approaches for identifying bacterial virulence factors.

His research in the lab focuses on developing data-driven and high-throughput methods to identify virulence factors, beginning with the highly diverse colonisation factors of enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC). By combining comparative genomics, bioinformatics, and machine learning, the project aims to improve our ability to detect and classify virulence-associated genes at scale.

We are excited to have Gleb join the team and look forward to the new methods and discoveries his work will bring.

Welcome to the lab, Gleb!
entry.summary: I am excited to welcome Gleb Ebert to the von Mentzer Lab as a postdoctoral researcher.

entry.title: Welcome Marina Masliakova to the von Mentzer Lab

link.title: Welcome Marina Masliakova to the von Mentzer Lab

entry.content:
I am very happy to welcome Marina Masliakova as the second PhD student in the von Mentzer Lab!

Marina joins the group as a doctoral student within the Data-Driven Life Science (DDLS) programme at the Department of Microbiology and Immunology, University of Gothenburg. With a background in bioinformatics and computational biology, she brings strong expertise in analysing large-scale biological data and is now applying these skills to questions in bacterial evolution and pathogenesis.

During her PhD, Marina will be entering the world of microbiology using computational approaches to study how Escherichia coli evolves through the acquisition and exchange of virulence factors. Using comparative genomics, machine learning, and evolutionary analyses, her project aims to understand how new pathogenic variants emerge, adapt to different hosts, and spread across bacterial populations.

We are excited to have Marina on board and look forward to seeing her develop as an interdisciplinary researcher over the coming years. Welcome to the team, Marina, and to the University of Gothenburg!

i also want to add a picture to the post

entry.title: Welcome Marlene Grimmer to the von Mentzer Lab

link.title: Welcome Marlene Grimmer to the von Mentzer Lab

entry.content:
I am very happy to welcome Marlene Grimmer as the first PhD student in the von Mentzer Lab!

Marlene joins the group as a doctoral student at the Department of Microbiology and Immunology, University of Gothenburg.

Her PhD project focuses on enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC), with a special interest in emerging colonisation factors. By combining computational and experimental approaches, Marlene will study how these important virulence factors evolve, spread, and contribute to bacterial adaptation and disease.

We are excited to have Marlene on board and look forward to following her research over the coming years.

Welcome to the team, Marlene!
entry.summary: I am very happy to welcome Marlene Grimmer as the first PhD student in the von Mentzer Lab!

entry.title: Welcome new postdoc - Biel Puigserver
entry.content:
We are very excited to announce that Biel Puigserver has joined the lab!
Biel comes from a veterinary background with expertise in both wet-and bioinformatics. He will be involved in computational projects investigating the Evolution of E. coli.
entry.summary: We are very excited to announce that Biel Puigserver has joined the lab! Biel comes from a veterinary background with expertise in both wet-and bioinformatics. He will be involved in computational projects investigating the Evolution of E. coli.

entry.title: Visiting ETH-Zurich and the Environmental Microbiology group

link.title: Visiting ETH-Zurich and the Environmental Microbiology group

entry.content:
Last year, Prof. Dr. Marie Schölmerich reached out for help with the shipment of samples collected from the pristine wetlands in Värmland. I was happy to assist a fellow female researcher, and after some logistical challenges—including a few lost samples—most of them finally arrived safely in Zurich.
Recently, I had the opportunity to visit Marie’s lab at ETH Zurich, where I gave a talk on colonisation factors in E. coli. It was a great chance to discuss ongoing research and explore potential collaborations on metagenomic sequencing of the Värmland samples. The data holds exciting potential for understanding microbial communities in these unique environments, and I’m looking forward to working together on this.
Beyond science, we also talked about the challenges of setting up a new lab as junior PIs—covering everything from recruitment to lab organisation. Marie shared invaluable insights, and I’ve already started implementing some of her advice in the von Mentzer Lab.
It was a fantastic visit filled with engaging discussions, new ideas, and valuable takeaways. Looking forward to future collaborations!




Read more on about the research in Schölmerich Lab)
entry.summary: ETH Zurich Last year, Prof. Dr. Marie Schölmerich reached out for help with the shipment of samples collected from the pristine wetlands in Värmland. I was happy to assist a fellow female researcher, and after some logistical challenges—including a few lost samples—most of them finally arrived safely in Zurich. Recently, I had the opportunity to visit Marie’s lab at ETH Zurich, where I gave a talk on colonisation factors in E. coli. It was a great chance to discuss ongoing research and explore potential collaborations on metagenomic sequencing of the Värmland samples. The data holds exciting potential for understanding microbial communities in these unique environments, and I’m looking forward to working together on this. Beyond science, we also talked about the challenges of setting up a new lab as junior PIs—covering everything from recruitment to lab organisation. Marie shared invaluable insights, and I’ve already started implementing some of her advice in the von Mentzer Lab. It was a fantastic visit filled with engaging discussions, new ideas, and valuable takeaways. Looking forward to future collaborations! Read more on about the research in Schölmerich Lab)

entry.title: Welcome Esmee and Melek - new students!

link.title: Welcome Esmee and Melek - new students!

entry.content:
We are delighted to welcome two new students to the von Mentzer Lab!
Esmee Shenkeveld - MSc-student
We are pleased to introduce our new MSc student, Esmee, who will be focusing on investigating a recently identified colonisation factor, CS23. Her research will delve into the plasmids carrying CS23 from isolates collected in Bolivia, spanning both patients and the environment. This important work aims to enhance our understanding of how CS23 contributes to E. coli colonisation and its implications for public health.
Melek Ebrar Emer - Erasmus-student
We are also thrilled to welcome an Erasmus student, Melek, who will be part of our lab for the next semester. Melek will be working on a large-scale project invetsigating the plasmidome of ETEC.
entry.summary: We are delighted to welcome two new students to the von Mentzer Lab! Esmee Shenkeveld - MSc-student We are pleased to introduce our new MSc student, Esmee, who will be focusing on investigating a recently identified colonisation factor, CS23. Her research will delve into the plasmids carrying CS23 from isolates collected in Bolivia, spanning both patients and the environment. This important work aims to enhance our understanding of how CS23 contributes to E. coli colonisation and its implications for public health. Melek Ebrar Emer - Erasmus-student We are also thrilled to welcome an Erasmus student, Melek, who will be part of our lab for the next semester. Melek will be working on a large-scale project invetsigating the plasmidome of ETEC.

entry.title: Keynote lecture at the ECMIS conference in Ghent

link.title: Keynote lecture at the ECMIS conference in Ghent

entry.content:
I’m are excited to share that I recently gave a keynote lecture at the E. coli and Mucosal Immune Systems (ECMIS) Conference in Ghent. The topic of the lecture was “Colonisation Factors in E. coli,” an area of research that is both fascinating and vital to understanding bacterial pathogenesis.
We delved into the history of the nomenclature of adhesins, shedding light on the complexities and evolution of the terminology over the years. We also discussed the first visualisation of adhesins using transmission electron microscopy, marking a significant milestone in microbiology.
Additionally, I presented a summary of the latest research from our lab, which investigates colonisation factors across E. coli using large-scale genetic analyses. This cutting-edge research is helping to uncover new insights into how these factors contribute to bacterial colonisation and infection.
It was really exciting to give my first keynote lecture and I was happy to see some old friends at the conference too!

Stay tuned for more updates and detailed findings from our ongoing research!
entry.summary: I’m are excited to share that I recently gave a keynote lecture at the E. coli and Mucosal Immune Systems (ECMIS) Conference in Ghent. The topic of the lecture was “Colonisation Factors in E. coli,” an area of research that is both fascinating and vital to understanding bacterial pathogenesis. We delved into the history of the nomenclature of adhesins, shedding light on the complexities and evolution of the terminology over the years. We also discussed the first visualisation of adhesins using transmission electron microscopy, marking a significant milestone in microbiology. Additionally, I presented a summary of the latest research from our lab, which investigates colonisation factors across E. coli using large-scale genetic analyses. This cutting-edge research is helping to uncover new insights into how these factors contribute to bacterial colonisation and infection. It was really exciting to give my first keynote lecture and I was happy to see some old friends at the conference too! Stay tuned for more updates and detailed findings from our ongoing research!

entry.title: von Mentzer Lab receives funding from Jeanssons Stiftelse

link.title: von Mentzer Lab receives funding from Jeanssons Stiftelse

entry.title: Postdoc Scholarship Available - Apply Now!
entry.content:
Are you interested in mocrobial genomics, virulence and antibiotic resistance?
Then you need to apply for the Postdoc Scholarship in the von Mentzer Lab!

Join us in experiencing the perfect blend of research and quality of life. Collaborate with leading minds, dive into innovative research, and create lasting connections within Sweden’s dynamic academic community.

Beyond academia, Sweden’s commitment to work-life balance ensures you’ll have time to explore the country’s captivating culture and natural beauty.

We can’t wait to welcome you to Sweden, where your academic journey will flourish.

Ready to seize this opportunity? Read more about our Research and the Team and how to apply here.
entry.summary: Are you interested in mocrobial genomics, virulence and antibiotic resistance? Then you need to apply for the Postdoc Scholarship in the von Mentzer Lab! Join us in experiencing the perfect blend of research and quality of life. Collaborate with leading minds, dive into innovative research, and create lasting connections within Sweden’s dynamic academic community. Beyond academia, Sweden’s commitment to work-life balance ensures you’ll have time to explore the country’s captivating culture and natural beauty. We can’t wait to welcome you to Sweden, where your academic journey will flourish. Ready to seize this opportunity? Read more about our Research and the Team and how to apply here.

entry.title: Exploring AMR - We will participate in the EMBO Plasmid Workshop

link.title: Exploring AMR - We will participate in the EMBO Plasmid Workshop

entry.content:
We’re excited to share a lab update! I am gearing up for the EMBO Workshop, a deep dive into plasmid evolution and its connection to antimicrobial resistance (AMR).
Decoding Antibiotic Resistance Spread
Antimicrobial resistance is a global challenge, with bacteria becoming resistant to antibiotics. Plasmids, tiny genetic carriers, play a role by shuttling resistance genes between bacteria.
Unveiling Plasmid Secrets
At the EMBO Workshop, we’ll be exploring plasmid biology and ecology. Recent tech advancements allow us to better understand how plasmids contribute to AMR outbreaks.
Collaboration for Progress The workshop is a hub of experts – from modelers to genetics researchers. I am thrilled to be part of this collaborative effort to address AMR challenges.
My Role in the Workshop
I’m eager to learn, contribute, and develop fresh approaches to tackle AMR. Stay tuned as we share insights from this enlightening experience.

Learn more about the workshop: EMBO Plasmid workshop
entry.summary: We’re excited to share a lab update! I am gearing up for the EMBO Workshop, a deep dive into plasmid evolution and its connection to antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Decoding Antibiotic Resistance Spread Antimicrobial resistance is a global challenge, with bacteria becoming resistant to antibiotics. Plasmids, tiny genetic carriers, play a role by shuttling resistance genes between bacteria. Unveiling Plasmid Secrets At the EMBO Workshop, we’ll be exploring plasmid biology and ecology. Recent tech advancements allow us to better understand how plasmids contribute to AMR outbreaks. Collaboration for Progress The workshop is a hub of experts – from modelers to genetics researchers. I am thrilled to be part of this collaborative effort to address AMR challenges. My Role in the Workshop I’m eager to learn, contribute, and develop fresh approaches to tackle AMR. Stay tuned as we share insights from this enlightening experience. Learn more about the workshop: EMBO Plasmid workshop
